sentences.base_language: Sophie had booked her TGV ticket weeks in advance, looking forward to visiting her family in Lyon.
sentences.target_language: Sophie avait réservé son billet de TGV des semaines à l'avance, impatiente de rendre visite à sa famille à Lyon.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Avait réservé** — plus-que-parfait expressing an action completed before the story's main timeline: **avait réservé** (had booked). The plus-que-parfait (**avait** + past participle) places the booking firmly before the departure morning described in the following sentences. Compare: **a réservé** (passé composé — booked at a specific moment in the narrative) vs **avait réservé** (plus-que-parfait — already done before the story begins).
**Impatiente de + infinitif** = eager to, looking forward to doing: **impatiente de** rendre visite (eager to visit). This adjective + **de** + infinitive construction expresses anticipation: **impatient/e de** partir (eager to leave), **ravi/e de** vous rencontrer (delighted to meet you). **Rendre visite à** = to visit (a person): **rendre visite à** sa famille (visit her family). Contrast: **visiter** (to visit a place): **visiter** Lyon (visit the city of Lyon). The distinction is important — **visiter** cannot take a person as its object.

sentences.base_language: On the morning of departure, she arrived at Gare de Lyon with a large bag and a mixture of excitement and nostalgia.
sentences.target_language: Le matin du départ, elle est arrivée à la Gare de Lyon avec un grand sac et un mélange d'excitation et de nostalgie.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Est arrivée** — **arriver** uses **être** in the passé composé, with past participle agreement: **est arrivée** (she arrived — feminine **-e** ending agreeing with **elle**). The group of verbs that take **être** as auxiliary includes **arriver, partir, entrer, sortir, monter, descendre, naître, mourir**, and all pronominal verbs. Agreement with the subject is obligatory: **il est arrivé** (m.) vs **elle est arrivée** (f.) vs **elles sont arrivées** (f. pl.).
**Un mélange de… et de…** = a mixture of… and…: **un mélange d'**excitation **et de** nostalgie. When **de** precedes a vowel it elides to **d'**: **d'**excitation. In this construction listing two items after **mélange de**, the preposition **de** is repeated before the second element: **un mélange de** joie **et de** tristesse (a mixture of joy and sadness). This repetition of the preposition is standard practice in French when coordinating noun complements.

sentences.base_language: The platform was crowded with travelers of all kinds hurrying to board.
sentences.target_language: Le quai était bondé de voyageurs de toutes sortes qui se pressaient pour monter à bord.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Était bondé** — imparfait used for a background description: **était bondé** (was crowded). The imparfait paints the scene that Sophie walked into — an ongoing state, not a completed event. Compare: **a été bondé** (passé composé — was crowded at one specific past moment, or 'turned out to be crowded') vs **était bondé** (imparfait — the platform's crowded state as background context for Sophie's arrival).
**Se presser pour + infinitif** = to hurry to do: **qui se pressaient pour** monter (who were hurrying to board). **Se presser** (pronominal) = to hurry, to rush: **pressez-vous** (hurry up). The relative clause **qui se pressaient** uses the imparfait because the hurrying was an ongoing, continuous activity in the background. **Monter à bord** = to board (a train, plane, or ship): **monter à bord du** TGV (board the TGV). **Monter dans** le train is also correct and common: **monter dans** le train (get on the train).

sentences.base_language: She found her reserved window seat and made herself comfortable with a paperback and some snacks.
sentences.target_language: Elle a trouvé sa place réservée côté fenêtre et s'est installée confortablement avec un livre de poche et quelques en-cas.
sentences.grammarNote:
**S'est installée** — pronominal verb **s'installer** with **être** in the passé composé, past participle agreeing with the subject: **s'est installée** (she made herself comfortable — feminine **-e**). **S'installer** = to settle in, to make oneself at home, to set up: **s'installer** dans un fauteuil (settle into an armchair). The reflexive pronoun **se** is an indirect object here, so the past participle agrees with the subject, not the object: **elle s'est installée** (the past participle agrees with **elle**, the subject).
**Quelques en-cas** — **quelques** + plural noun = some, a few: **quelques** en-cas (some snacks). **Quelques** always precedes a plural noun and implies a small, indefinite number: **quelques** minutes (a few minutes), **quelques** amis (a few friends). **En-cas** (m., invariable) = snack, something to eat just in case — a compound noun from **en cas de besoin** (in case of need): **un en-cas** (a snack), **des en-cas** (snacks). The hyphen is obligatory and the word does not add a plural **-s**.

sentences.base_language: As the TGV pulled out of Paris, the grey Parisian suburbs gradually gave way to open fields and rolling countryside.
sentences.target_language: Au fur et à mesure que le TGV quittait Paris, les grises banlieues parisiennes laissaient peu à peu place à des champs ouverts et à un paysage vallonné.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Au fur et à mesure que + imparfait** = as, gradually as: **au fur et à mesure que** le TGV **quittait** Paris (as the TGV was pulling out of Paris). **Au fur et à mesure que** expresses gradual, progressive change and is paired with the imparfait to convey continuous, simultaneous development. It is richer than the simple **comme** or **alors que**: **au fur et à mesure que** le temps passait (as time went by). Both clauses use imparfait for parallel ongoing action.
**Laisser place à** = to give way to, to make way for: **laissaient… place à** des champs ouverts (gave way to open fields). **Laisser place à** is a fixed expression: la pluie a laissé **place à** un ciel bleu (the rain gave way to a blue sky). **Vallonné/e** = rolling, hilly — from **vallon** (small valley): **un paysage vallonné** (rolling countryside). **Peu à peu** = gradually, little by little — a common adverbial phrase synonymous with **progressivement** and **graduellement** but more idiomatic in everyday French.

sentences.base_language: Sophie felt a wave of calm settle over her — something about train travel always had that effect on her.
sentences.target_language: Sophie a senti une vague de calme l'envahir — quelque chose dans le voyage en train avait toujours cet effet sur elle.
sentences.grammarNote:
**A senti + infinitif** — perception verb **sentir** + infinitive expresses sensing an action take place: **a senti une vague… l'envahir** (felt a wave… wash over her). Verbs of perception (**voir, entendre, sentir, regarder, écouter**) can be followed by a noun + infinitive to describe witnessing an action: elle **a entendu** la porte **se fermer** (she heard the door close), il **a vu** son ami **partir** (he saw his friend leave). The noun is the subject of the infinitive action.
**Avait toujours cet effet sur elle** — imparfait for a habitual or general truth in the past: **avait toujours** cet effet (always had that effect — a recurring, habitual reality). The imparfait is used for actions or states that were habitual, repeated, or characteristically true over a period: **le train lui avait toujours fait cet effet** (the train always had that effect on her — a longstanding habit). Compare: **a eu cet effet** (passé composé — had that effect on one specific occasion).

sentences.base_language: After about an hour, the woman sitting across from her struck up a friendly conversation.
sentences.target_language: Au bout d'une heure environ, la femme assise en face d'elle a engagé une conversation amicale.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Au bout de + durée** = after (a period of time), at the end of: **au bout d'**une heure (after an hour). **Au bout de** indicates that a period of time has fully elapsed before the next action: **au bout de** trois jours (after three days), **au bout d'**un moment (after a while). Compare: **après** + noun/infinitive: **après** une heure (after an hour — interchangeable here). **Au bout de** has a slight nuance of 'at the end of a stretch of time' and is very natural in narrative.
**Engager une conversation** = to strike up a conversation: **a engagé une conversation** amicale (struck up a friendly conversation). **Engager** in this context is formal and idiomatic: **engager la conversation** (to start talking, to open a conversation). Compare: **entamer une conversation** (to begin a conversation — also formal), **se mettre à parler** (to start talking — more casual). **Assise en face d'elle** — adjective **assise** (seated, sitting — past participle of **s'asseoir** used as adjective, feminine to agree with **la femme**): **la femme assise** en face (the woman sitting opposite).

sentences.base_language: She turned out to be a retired schoolteacher who took this route every month to see her grandchildren.
sentences.target_language: Elle s'est avérée être une institutrice à la retraite qui empruntait cet itinéraire chaque mois pour voir ses petits-enfants.
sentences.grammarNote:
**S'avérer être** = to turn out to be, to prove to be: **s'est avérée être** une institutrice (turned out to be a teacher). **S'avérer** (pronominal) is used with an adjective or a noun to express what something proves to be: **s'avérer** difficile (to prove difficult), **s'avérer** être un expert (to turn out to be an expert). The past participle **avérée** agrees with the subject **elle** (feminine). Note: **s'avérer** takes **être** as auxiliary in compound tenses, and agreement with the subject is obligatory.
**Empruntait** — imparfait for a habitual past action: **empruntait cet itinéraire chaque mois** (took this route every month — a regular, repeated activity). The imparfait is the standard tense for expressing habitual or repeated actions in the past. Markers like **chaque mois** (every month), **tous les jours** (every day), **souvent** (often) strongly signal the imparfait. **Emprunter** = to take (a route), to use (a path): **emprunter** une route (to take a road), **emprunter** le couloir (to go through the corridor) — distinct from **emprunter** (to borrow), which is the same verb but context-dependent.

sentences.base_language: Sophie's stomach growled softly and she realized she hadn't eaten anything since early morning.
sentences.target_language: L'estomac de Sophie a gargouillé doucement et elle a réalisé qu'elle n'avait rien mangé depuis le début de la matinée.
sentences.grammarNote:
**N'avait rien mangé** — plus-que-parfait in a subordinate clause: **qu'elle n'avait rien mangé** (that she hadn't eaten anything). The plus-que-parfait expresses an action that pre-dated the realization: she realized (passé composé) that she had not eaten (plus-que-parfait — before that moment). **Ne… rien** = nothing, not anything: **n'avait rien mangé** (had eaten nothing). **Rien** functions as the direct object and is placed between the auxiliary and the past participle: n'**avait** rien **mangé** (not *n'avait mangé rien*).
**Depuis + nom** = since (a point in time): **depuis le début de la matinée** (since early morning). **Depuis** + a point in time indicates when something started: **depuis** ce matin (since this morning), **depuis** hier (since yesterday). Contrast with **depuis** + duration: **depuis** deux heures (for two hours). With a negative verb in the past, **depuis** expresses a duration of non-action: elle **n'avait rien mangé depuis** le début de la matinée (she had eaten nothing since early morning — the non-eating stretched from then until the moment of realization).

sentences.base_language: She made her way to the bar car and ordered a hot croque-monsieur and a coffee.
sentences.target_language: Elle s'est dirigée vers la voiture-bar et a commandé un croque-monsieur chaud et un café.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Se diriger vers** = to make one's way to, to head toward: **s'est dirigée vers** la voiture-bar (made her way to the bar car). **Se diriger** is a pronominal verb of motion: **se diriger vers** (to head toward), **se diriger dans la direction de** (to head in the direction of). Past participle **dirigée** agrees with the subject **elle** (feminine). Compare: **aller vers** (to go toward — simple, neutral), **se diriger vers** (to head toward — implies deliberate, directed movement).
**Voiture-bar** (f.) = bar car, dining car on a French train: **la voiture-bar** (the bar car). French train vocabulary: **la voiture** or **le wagon** (carriage/car), **la voiture-bar** (bar car), **la voiture-restaurant** (dining car), **le contrôleur/la contrôleuse** (conductor), **le couloir** (aisle/corridor), **le compartiment** (compartment), **la place réservée** (reserved seat). **Un croque-monsieur** (m.) = a toasted ham-and-cheese sandwich, the iconic French snack: **un croque-monsieur** (with ham and cheese), **un croque-madame** (same but topped with a fried egg).

sentences.base_language: The simple food tasted surprisingly good, perhaps because of the landscape rushing past outside the window.
sentences.target_language: La nourriture simple avait un goût étonnamment bon, peut-être à cause du paysage qui défilait dehors la fenêtre.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Avoir un goût + adjectif** = to taste (a certain way): **avait un goût** étonnamment bon (tasted surprisingly good). **Avoir bon goût** = to taste good (general), but **avoir un goût** + adjective describes a specific flavor quality: **avoir un goût** amer (to taste bitter), **avoir un goût** de citron (to taste of lemon). The imparfait **avait** is used here because the tasting experience was ongoing during the journey, not a punctual event. Compare: **goûter** + adjective (in Quebec French: ça **goûte** bon — it tastes good), which is non-standard in European French.
**Qui défilait** — imparfait in a relative clause for ongoing background action: **le paysage qui défilait** (the landscape that was rushing past / scrolling by). **Défiler** = to scroll past, to file past, to parade: le paysage **défile** (the landscape rushes by — images or scenes passing in succession). The imparfait **défilait** expresses the continuous, uninterrupted motion of the scenery as a background state. Compare: **qui a défilé** (passé composé — which rushed past at one specific moment).

sentences.base_language: Back at her seat, she dozed off briefly and woke up just as the train passed through a long tunnel.
sentences.target_language: De retour à sa place, elle a somnolé un moment et s'est réveillée juste au moment où le train traversait un long tunnel.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Juste au moment où + imparfait** = just as, right at the moment when: **juste au moment où** le train **traversait** un tunnel (just as the train was passing through a tunnel). The imparfait **traversait** expresses the ongoing action (the train was in the process of going through the tunnel) that frames the completed event of waking up (passé composé: **s'est réveillée**). This contrast — passé composé for the interrupting event + imparfait for the ongoing background — is the classic narrative use of these two tenses.
**S'est réveillée** — pronominal verb **se réveiller** with **être** in the passé composé: **s'est réveillée** (woke up — feminine agreement). **Se réveiller** = to wake up (intransitive, pronominal). Compare: **réveiller quelqu'un** (to wake someone up — transitive, takes **avoir**): elle **a réveillé** son voisin (she woke her neighbor up). **De retour à** = back at, having returned to: **de retour à** sa place (back at her seat). A concise, idiomatic way to indicate return: **de retour au** bureau (back at the office), **de retour à** Paris (back in Paris).

sentences.base_language: The conductor came through the carriage to check tickets and announced the next stop in twenty minutes.
sentences.target_language: Le contrôleur est passé dans la voiture pour vérifier les billets et a annoncé que le prochain arrêt aurait lieu dans vingt minutes.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Aurait lieu** — conditional used in reported speech (futur du passé): **a annoncé que… aurait lieu** (announced that… would take place). The original announcement was **le prochain arrêt aura lieu** (the next stop will be — future indicative). In reported speech after a past main verb, the future indicative becomes the conditional: **aura lieu** → **aurait lieu**. This shift of tenses in reported speech (**concordance des temps**) is systematic in formal French.
**Avoir lieu** = to take place, to occur: **le prochain arrêt aurait lieu dans vingt minutes** (the next stop would be in twenty minutes). **Avoir lieu** is a fixed expression used for events and occurrences: **la réunion a eu lieu hier** (the meeting took place yesterday), **le concert aura lieu demain** (the concert will take place tomorrow). **Est passé dans** — **passer dans** = to come through, to pass through (a space): **est passé dans** la voiture (came through the carriage). **Passer** uses **être** in the passé composé when intransitive and meaning to pass/go through.

sentences.base_language: Sophie's back ached from sitting so long and she stood up to stretch her legs in the aisle.
sentences.target_language: Le dos de Sophie lui faisait mal à force de rester assise si longtemps et elle s'est levée pour se dégourdir les jambes dans le couloir.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Faire mal à** = to hurt, to ache: **le dos lui faisait mal** (her back ached — lit. the back was hurting her). **Faire mal à quelqu'un** is the standard construction for physical pain: **le genou lui fait mal** (her knee hurts), **ça me fait mal** (it hurts me). The body part is the subject and the person is the indirect object: **le dos lui faisait mal** (her back was hurting her — **lui** = to her). Compare: **avoir mal à** = to have a pain in: **elle avait mal au dos** (her back hurt — she had back pain — equally correct and common).
**Se dégourdir les jambes** = to stretch one's legs: **se dégourdir les jambes** dans le couloir (stretch her legs in the aisle). **Se dégourdir** (pronominal) = to loosen up, to work the stiffness out: **se dégourdir** les membres (to limber up). The pattern **se** + verb + definite article + body part is standard for reflexive actions on one's own body: **se laver les mains** (wash one's hands), **se dégourdir les jambes** (stretch one's legs) — the possessive is not used before the body part when a reflexive pronoun is present.

sentences.base_language: A small boy in the seat ahead was pressing his nose against the glass, delighted each time he spotted a cow.
sentences.target_language: Un petit garçon dans le siège devant elle appuyait son nez contre la vitre, ravi chaque fois qu'il apercevait une vache.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Appuyait** — imparfait for a vivid, ongoing action in the narrative background: **appuyait son nez contre la vitre** (was pressing his nose against the glass). The imparfait presents the boy's action as a continuous, repeated scene rather than a one-time event. This use of the imparfait — picturing an action in progress, like a film freeze-frame — is called the imparfait de description or imparfait pittoresque.
**Chaque fois que + indicatif** = each time, every time: **chaque fois qu'**il **apercevait** une vache (each time he spotted a cow). **Chaque fois que** expresses habitual or repeated condition and takes the indicative. The imparfait **apercevait** indicates a repeated past action: each time it happened. **Apercevoir** = to catch sight of, to spot: **apercevoir** une vache (to spot a cow), **j'ai aperçu** un ami dans la rue (I spotted a friend in the street). **Apercevoir** implies a brief or sudden visual perception, more fleeting than **voir** (to see).

sentences.base_language: Sophie smiled and thought about her own childhood journeys on this line with her parents.
sentences.target_language: Sophie a souri et a pensé à ses propres voyages d'enfance sur cette ligne avec ses parents.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Penser à** = to think about (in the sense of directing one's thoughts at something): **a pensé à** ses voyages d'enfance (thought about her childhood journeys). **Penser à** vs **penser de**: **penser à** quelque chose = to think about, to have in mind (direction of thought): **je pense à** toi (I'm thinking of you). **Penser de** quelque chose = to think of, to have an opinion about: **qu'est-ce que tu en penses?** (what do you think of it? — **en** replaces **de** + thing). The distinction is important and frequently tested.
**Ses propres voyages** — **propre** placed before the noun means own (emphasizing possession): **ses propres** voyages (her own journeys). **Propre** has two meanings depending on its position: before the noun — **propre** = own (**sa propre** maison = her own house); after the noun — **propre** = clean (**une maison propre** = a clean house). This is one of the classic examples of French adjectives that change meaning with position, alongside **ancien** (former / old), **certain** (specific / sure), **grand** (great / tall), **même** (same / itself).

sentences.base_language: As the TGV began to slow on approach to Lyon Part-Dieu, she started gathering her things with growing anticipation.
sentences.target_language: Au moment où le TGV a commencé à ralentir à l'approche de Lyon Part-Dieu, elle s'est mise à rassembler ses affaires avec une impatience croissante.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Se mettre à + infinitif** = to start doing, to begin to do: **s'est mise à** rassembler (started gathering). **Se mettre à** emphasizes the beginning of an action, often with a sense of immediacy or decisiveness: **elle s'est mise à** pleurer (she started crying), **il s'est mis à** courir (he started running). Past participle **mise** agrees with the subject **elle** (feminine). Compare: **commencer à** + infinitive (also means to start — more neutral), **se mettre à** is more vivid and often signals a sudden or energetic start.
**À l'approche de** = on approach to, as one nears: **à l'approche de** Lyon (on approach to Lyon). This prepositional phrase conveys the moment of drawing near: **à l'approche de** l'hiver (as winter approached), **à l'approche de** la retraite (approaching retirement). **Ralentir** = to slow down (intransitive or transitive): le train **a ralenti** (the train slowed down — intransitive), **ralentir** sa voiture (to slow one's car — transitive). **Impatience croissante** = growing anticipation — **croissant/e** = growing, increasing (present participle of **croître** used as adjective): une impatience **croissante** (mounting impatience).

sentences.base_language: She could already picture her mother and sister waving on the platform.
sentences.target_language: Elle imaginait déjà sa mère et sa sœur qui lui faisaient signe sur le quai.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Imaginait** — imparfait for a mental state or ongoing imagining: **elle imaginait déjà** (she was already picturing). Verbs expressing mental states, feelings, and perceptions (**penser, croire, imaginer, espérer, savoir, vouloir, pouvoir**) typically use the imparfait when describing an ongoing mental condition in the past. The imparfait here gives a sense of Sophie already living the moment in her mind as the train approached.
**Faire signe à quelqu'un** = to wave to someone, to signal to someone: **qui lui faisaient signe** (who were waving to her). **Faire signe** is the standard expression for waving as a greeting or signal: **faire signe de la main** (to wave with one's hand), **faire signe que oui** (to nod yes), **faire signe que non** (to shake one's head no). **Lui faisaient signe** — indirect object pronoun **lui** (to her): **faire signe à** quelqu'un → **lui faire signe** (to wave to her/him). The indirect object pronoun precedes the conjugated verb.

sentences.base_language: The train arrived exactly on time and Sophie stepped onto the platform feeling stiff but deeply happy.
sentences.target_language: Le train est arrivé exactement à l'heure et Sophie est descendue sur le quai, les muscles un peu raidis mais profondément heureuse.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Est descendue** — **descendre** uses **être** in the passé composé with agreement: **est descendue** (stepped down, got off — feminine). **Descendre** is one of the core **être** verbs: **monter** (go up / get on), **descendre** (go down / get off), **entrer** (go in), **sortir** (go out). However, when **descendre** is used transitively (with a direct object), it takes **avoir**: elle **a descendu** ses bagages (she brought her bags down — transitive). The auxiliary choice (être vs avoir) changes the meaning of some verbs.
**Les muscles un peu raidis** = with muscles a little stiff — the structure [definite article] + [noun] + [adjective/participle] expresses an accompanying manner or state: **les muscles raidis** (with stiff muscles), **les yeux brillants** (with shining eyes), **le cœur léger** (light-hearted). This characteristically French construction conveys a simultaneous state without using **avec** + possessive: **les muscles raidis** rather than *avec ses muscles raidis*. **Raidi/e** = stiffened, stiff — past participle of **raidir** used as adjective.

sentences.base_language: Walking toward the exit with her bag, she decided that however convenient planes were, nothing could replace the pleasure of traveling by train.
sentences.target_language: En marchant vers la sortie avec son sac, elle a décidé que, si pratiques que soient les avions, rien ne pourrait remplacer le plaisir de voyager en train.
sentences.grammarNote:
**En marchant** — gérondif expressing a simultaneous action: **en marchant vers la sortie** (while walking toward the exit). The gérondif (**en** + present participle) here indicates that the decision was made during the act of walking. The subject of the gérondif must be the same as the subject of the main verb: **elle** was walking and **elle** decided. Form: **en** + present participle (nous-stem + **-ant**): **en marchant** (while walking), **en réfléchissant** (while reflecting).
**Si… que + subjonctif** = however… (concessive): **si pratiques que soient** les avions (however convenient planes may be). This construction — **si** + adjective + **que** + subjunctive — is an elegant, formal way to express concession: **si difficile que soit** la tâche (however difficult the task may be), **si loin que ce soit** (however far it may be). The subjunctive **soient** (third person plural of **être**) is obligatory in this construction. **Ne… que** = only, nothing but: **rien ne pourrait remplacer** (nothing could replace) — here **rien… ne** is the subject negation, and **le plaisir de + infinitif** = the pleasure of doing: **le plaisir de** voyager (the pleasure of traveling).
